q: Describe the primary function of the cardiovascular system.
a: The primary function is the transport of materials to and from all parts of the body.

q: Give examples of materials entering the body that are transported by the cardiovascular system.
a: Oxygen is transferred from the lungs to all cells. Nutrients and water are transferred from the intestinal tract to all cells.

q: Give examples of materials moved from cell to cell by the cardiovascular system.
a: Wastes are transferred to the liver for processing. Immune cells, antibodies, and clotting proteins are continuously available. Hormones are transferred from endocrine cells to target cells. Stored nutrients are transferred from the liver and adipose tissue to all cells.

q: Give examples of materials leaving the body that are transported by the cardiovascular system.
a: Metabolic wastes are transferred to the kidneys for elimination. Heat is transferred to the skin for dissipation. Carbon dioxide is transferred to the lungs for elimination.

q: Describe the organization of the cardiovascular system, starting and ending in the aorta.
a: Blood flows from the aorta into smaller arteries to distribute oxygenated blood to capillary networks in tissues. Blood exchanges material at capillaries and returns via veins to the superior and inferior vena cava. The vena cavae enter the right atrium, which sends blood to the right ventricle. The right ventricle pumps blood through pulmonary arteries to the lungs for oxygenation. Pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood to the left atrium, which sends it to the left ventricle. The left ventricle pumps blood back out into the aorta.

q: Define the keyword: capillaries. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Microscopic vessels in tissues. Function: Site where blood exchanges material with the interstitial fluid.

q: Define the keyword: arteries.
a: Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart.

q: Define the keyword: veins.
a: Blood vessels that return blood to the heart.

q: Define the keyword: septum. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Central wall of the heart. Function: Divides the heart into independent left and right halves and prevents blood mixing.

q: Define the keyword: atria. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Upper chambers of the heart. Function: Receives blood returning to the heart from the blood vessels.

q: Define the keyword: ventricle. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Lower chambers of the heart. Function: Pumps blood out into the blood vessels.

q: Define the keyword: pulmonary arteries. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Connect right ventricle to lungs. Function: Carry low-oxygen blood to the lungs.

q: Define the keyword: pulmonary veins. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Connect lungs to left atrium. Function: Carry well-oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left heart.

q: Define the keyword: pulmonary circulation.
a: The blood vessels that go from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left atrium.

q: Define the keyword: systemic circulation.
a: The blood vessels that carry blood from the left side of the heart to the tissues and back to the right side of the heart.

q: Define the keyword: aorta. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Large artery exiting the left ventricle. Function: Branches into smaller arteries to distribute oxygenated blood to capillary networks in tissues.

q: Define the keyword: superior vena cava. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Vein entering the right atrium. Function: Returns blood from the upper part of the body.

q: Define the keyword: inferior vena cava. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Vein entering the right atrium. Function: Returns blood from the lower part of the body.

q: Know the flow of circulation of blood within the pulmonary circulation.
a:
[Image of pulmonary circulation flow]
 Blood travels from the right ventricle through the pulmonary arteries to the lungs. In the lungs, blood is oxygenated. Blood then travels through the pulmonary veins to the left atrium.

q: Know the flow of circulation of blood within the systemic circulation.
a:
[Image of systemic circulation flow]
 Blood travels from the left side of the heart through the aorta and smaller arteries to tissue capillaries. Deoxygenated blood returns from the tissues via veins like the superior and inferior vena cava back to the right side of the heart.

q: Describe in detail the internal and external anatomy of the heart.
a: The heart is encased in a membranous sac called the pericardium. The thick muscular walls are the myocardium. Internally, the heart is divided into four chambers separated by a septum. Atrioventricular valves separate the atria and ventricles. Semilunar valves separate ventricles from major arteries.

q: Define the keyword: pericardium. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Membranous sac encasing the heart. Function: Protects the heart and contains a thin layer of pericardial fluid that lubricates the external surface as it beats.

q: Define the keyword: myocardium. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Thick muscular walls of the heart. Function: Cardiac muscle responsible for contraction.

q: Define the keyword: atrioventricular valves. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Between the atria and ventricles. Function: Ensure one-way flow from atria to ventricles and prevent backward flow.

q: Define the keyword: semilunar valves. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Between the ventricles and the major arteries. Function: Snap closed when blood attempts to flow back into ventricles from arteries.

q: Define the keyword: papillary muscles. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Moundlike extensions of ventricular muscle. Function: Tether chordae tendineae and provide stability, but do not actively open or close valves.

q: Define the keyword: tricuspid valve. Include Location.
a: Location: Right AV valve that separates the right atrium and right ventricle, featuring three flaps.

q: Define the keyword: bicuspid valve.
a: Also known as the Mitral valve. It is the Left AV valve that separates the left atrium and left ventricle and has two flaps.

q: Define the keyword: mitral valve. Include Location.
a: Location: Left AV valve separating the left atrium and left ventricle. It is the same as the bicuspid valve.

q: Define the keyword: aortic valve. Include Location.
a: Location: Between left ventricle and aorta.

q: Define the keyword: pulmonary valve. Include Location.
a: Location: Between right ventricle and pulmonary trunk.

q: Define the keyword: coronary circulation.
a: The heart's dedicated blood supply, required due to high energy and oxygen demands.

q: Define the keyword: coronary arteries. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Branches originating from the aorta. Function: Arteries that nourish the heart muscle itself.

q: Define the keyword: coronary veins.
a: Veins that collect blood from capillaries in the heart muscle and empty it directly into the right atrium at the coronary sinus.

q: Define the keyword: coronary sinus. Include Location and Function.
a: Location: Posterior aspect of the heart. Function: Drains about 85% of venous blood from the myocardium directly into the right atrium.

q: Explain the importance of the valves of the heart.
a: Valves ensure one-way flow of blood through the heart. Atrioventricular valves prevent backward flow from ventricles to atria. Semilunar valves snap closed to prevent blood from flowing back into ventricles from the major arteries.

q: Describe the contractile cells of the heart, their functions, and their arrangement.
a: Contractile cells are typical striated muscle cells organized into sarcomeres. Function: They generate the mechanical force of the heart. Arrangement: Individual cells branch and join end-to-end to create a complex network connected by intercalated disks.

q: Describe the autorhythmic cells of the heart, their functions, and their arrangement.
a: Autorhythmic cells, also known as pacemakers, are specialized, anatomically distinct cells making up 1% of myocardial cells. Function: They generate action potentials spontaneously because the signal for contraction is myogenic. Arrangement: They are smaller, contain few contractile fibers, and have no organized sarcomeres.

q: Explain the key role of pacemaker cells in initiating action potentials without innervation.
a: Pacemaker cells have an unstable membrane potential called a pacemaker potential that slowly drifts up toward threshold. This allows them to spontaneously generate action potentials without needing a signal from the nervous system.

q: Diagram and explain the process of EC-coupling within cardiac muscle.
a: 1. Action potential enters a contractile cell from an adjacent cell via gap junctions. 2. Action potential moves across sarcolemma and into t-tubules, opening voltage-gated L-type Ca2+ channels. 3. Ca2+ enters the cell down its electrochemical gradient. 4. Calcium-induced calcium release (CICR) opens RyR Ca2+ release channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum. 5. Stored Ca2+ flows out of the SR into the cytosol. 6. Multiple sparks sum to create a large Ca2+ signal. 7. Ca2+ diffuses and binds to troponin, initiating crossbridge formation and contraction.

q: Define the keyword: Ca2+-inducted Ca2+ release (CICR).
a: A process where extracellular calcium entry opens ryanodine receptor (RyR) Ca2+ release channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

q: Describe the role of RyR channels in myocardial excitation-contraction coupling.
a: RyR channels are calcium release channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum that are opened by calcium entering the cell (CICR), allowing stored Ca2+ to flow out into the cytosol to create a Ca2+ spark.

q: Describe the role of the Na+-Ca2+ antiporter (NCX) in myocardial relaxation.
a: During relaxation, Ca2+ is removed from the cell by the Na+-Ca2+ exchanger (NCX) antiporter. It moves 1 Ca2+ out of the cell against its gradient for every 3 Na+ moving in down their gradient.

q: Describe the role of the sodium-potassium pump (Na+-K+-ATPase) in myocardial relaxation.
a: The Na+ that entered the cell via the NCX antiporter is pumped back out by the Na+-K+-ATPase to maintain ion concentration gradients.

q: Diagram and describe the phases of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Phase 4: Resting membrane potential, stable at about -90 mV. Phase 0: Depolarization as voltage-gated Na+ channels open and Na+ rapidly enters. Phase 1: Initial repolarization as Na+ channels close and fast K+ channels open. Phase 2: The plateau caused by voltage-gated Ca2+ channels opening (Ca2+ enters) and fast K+ channels closing. Phase 3: Rapid repolarization as Ca2+ channels close and slow K+ channels open allowing K+ efflux.

q: Explain refractory periods and summation in cardiac muscle and its relationship to tetanus.
a: The extended plateau phase means the refractory period lasts almost as long as the entire muscle twitch. The cell has almost completely relaxed by the time a second action potential can fire. This prevents summation and the fatal condition of tetanus.

q: Diagram and describe the action potentials of cardiac autorhythmic cells.
a: The pacemaker potential starts at -60 mV. If channels open allowing a net influx of Na+, slowly depolarizing the cell. If channels close and one set of Ca2+ channels opens to reach threshold. At threshold, a different subtype of voltage-gated Ca2+ channels opens, creating a steep depolarization. At the peak, Ca2+ channels close and slow K+ channels open to repolarize the cell back to -60 mV.

q: Define the keyword: pacemaker potential.
a: An unstable membrane potential in autorhythmic cells that starts at -60 mV and slowly drifts up toward threshold.

q: Define the keyword: If channels.
a: Funny channels belonging to the HCN family that open at -60 mV. They are permeable to both K+ and Na+, but Na+ influx exceeds K+ efflux.

q: Describe the conduction of electrical signals through the heart.
a:
[Image of electrical conduction pathway of the heart]
 The Sinoatrial (SA) node fires an action potential. The internodal pathway spreads the signal across the atria to the AV node. The AV node slows the transmission. The signal moves down the AV bundle in the ventricular septum. It splits into bundle branches continuing to the apex. Purkinje fibers transmit the signal rapidly outward among contractile cells at the apex.

q: Define the keyword: AV node delay.
a: The AV node slows down transmission of the action potential to allow atria to complete their contraction before ventricles begin.

q: Diagram and explain the cardiac cycle, its steps, and the physical actions of the heart during each.
a: The cardiac cycle consists of five main phases: Late Diastole (passive ventricular filling), Atrial Systole (active ventricular filling), Isovolumic Ventricular Contraction (ventricular pressure rises, AV valves close), Ventricular Ejection (semilunar valves open, blood is pumped out), and Isovolumic Ventricular Relaxation (ventricles relax, semilunar valves close).

q: Define the keyword: diastole.
a: The time during which cardiac muscle relaxes.

q: Define the keyword: systole.
a: The time during which cardiac muscle contracts.

q: Define the keyword: late diastole.
a: A phase of the cardiac cycle where both atria and ventricles are relaxed, AV valves are open, and blood flows passively by gravity from veins into atria and then into ventricles.

q: Define the keyword: atrial systole.
a: A phase following the P wave where the atria contract, forcing the last 20% of blood into the ventricles.

q: Define the keyword: isovolumic ventricular contraction.
a: A phase where ventricular systole begins, pressure in ventricles rises forcing AV valves closed, but semilunar valves remain closed, causing ventricles to contract isometrically without a volume change.

q: Define the keyword: ventricular ejection.
a: A phase where ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure in the aorta/pulmonary trunk, causing the semilunar valves to open and blood to be ejected.

q: Define the keyword: isovolumic ventricular relaxation.
a: A phase where ventricles begin to relax and repolarize (T wave), ventricular pressure decreases, and blood flows backward from arteries to snap the semilunar valves closed while AV valves remain closed.

q: Define the keyword: ventricular systole.
a: The period of ventricular contraction, beginning during the isovolumic ventricular contraction phase.

q: Define the keyword: ventricular diastole.
a: The period of ventricular relaxation, occurring during the T-P segment of an ECG.

q: Define the keyword: end-diastolic volume (EDV).
a: The maximum volume of blood the ventricles contain at the end of relaxation/filling, which averages about 135 mL.

q: Define the keyword: first heart sound.
a: The "lub" sound created by vibrations following the closure of the AV valves.

q: Define the keyword: end systolic volume (ESV).
a: The minimum volume of blood left in the ventricle at the end of contraction, which averages about 65 mL.

q: Define the keyword: second heart sound.
a: The "dup" sound created by vibrations from semilunar valve closure.

q: Describe the parts of an ECG and explain how these electrical events are related to the mechanical events of the cardiac cycle.
a:
An ECG is a surface recording of summed electrical activity. It features waves (depolarization/repolarization) and segments (delays/quiet periods). These electrical events trigger the mechanical events of systole (contraction) and diastole (relaxation). 

[Image of normal electrocardiogram wave showing P, QRS, and T segments]

q: Define the keyword: Segments (ECG).
a: Sections of the ECG recording that represent specific periods between waves, such as the P-R segment or T-P segment, often denoting conduction delays or electrically quiet periods.

q: Define the keyword: P wave.
a: A wave on the ECG that represents atrial depolarization.

q: Define the keyword: P-R segment.
a: Represents conduction through the AV node and AV bundle (the AV node delay). Atrial contraction occurs here.

q: Define the keywords: Q wave, R wave, S wave.
a: Components that make up the QRS complex, collectively representing the progressive wave of ventricular depolarization.

q: Define the keyword: QRS complex.
a: Represents the progressive wave of ventricular depolarization. Atrial repolarization is hidden within this complex.

q: Define the keyword: T wave.
a: A wave on the ECG that represents ventricular repolarization.

q: Define the keyword: S-T segment.
a: A segment on the ECG where depression often indicates decreased blood flow to a small area of the heart, known as ischemia or Non-STEMI.

q: Explain the pressure changes that occur during the cardiac cycle and their relationship to flow through the heart and blood vessels.
a: Fluids flow down pressure gradients. As ventricles contract (systole), pressure rises, closing AV valves. Once ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure, semilunar valves open to drive blood flow out. When ventricles relax, pressure drops below arterial pressure, closing semilunar valves, and eventually drops below atrial pressure to allow blood to flow in and refill the chamber.

q: Explain the relationship of heart rate, cardiac output, and stroke volume.
a: Cardiac Output (CO) is an indicator of total blood flow through the body. It is determined by multiplying the Heart Rate (beats per minute) by the Stroke Volume (amount of blood pumped per beat).

q: Define the keyword: stroke volume (SV).
a: The amount of blood pumped by one ventricle during one contraction.

q: Define the keyword: ejection fraction.
a: The percentage of EDV ejected with one contraction.

q: Define the keyword: cardiac output (CO).
a: The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle in a given period of time, serving as an indicator of total blood flow through the body.

q: Know how to do the calculations for stroke volume (SV).
a: Stroke Volume is calculated by subtracting End-Systolic Volume from End-Diastolic Volume. Equation: SV = EDV - ESV.

q: Know how to do the calculations for ejection fraction.
a: Ejection Fraction is calculated by dividing Stroke Volume by End-Diastolic Volume. Equation: Ejection Fraction = SV / EDV.

q: Know how to do the calculations for cardiac output (CO).
a: Cardiac Output is calculated by multiplying Heart Rate by Stroke Volume. Equation: CO = Heart Rate x Stroke Volume.

q: Explain the role of the parasympathetic autonomic division in control of heart rate at the cellular level.
a: Parasympathetic neurons release Acetylcholine (ACh), which binds to muscarinic cholinergic receptors on autorhythmic cells. This increases K+ efflux and decreases Ca2+ influx, which hyperpolarizes the cell and slows the rate of pacemaker depolarization. The cell takes longer to reach threshold, slowing the heart rate.

q: Explain the role of the sympathetic autonomic division in control of heart rate at the cellular level.
a: Sympathetic neurons release Norepinephrine, which binds to $\beta_{1}$-adrenergic receptors on autorhythmic cells, activating a cAMP second messenger system. cAMP binds to open $I_{f}$ channels, keeping them open longer to increase Na+ and Ca2+ influx. This speeds up the rate of pacemaker depolarization, reaching threshold faster and increasing the heart rate.

q: END
a: END
